entries.title: Coca-Cola, 1948
entries.description: "The pause that refreshes" — this classic lithograph ad depicts a soda jerk in a gleaming diner, presenting an ice-cold bottle of Coke to a smiling customer. The red-and-white palette became synonymous with American optimism in the postwar era.

entries.title: Pullman Travel, 1932
entries.description: A Depression-era rail ad promises glamour and escape aboard the Pullman sleeper cars. The streamlined locomotive races across an idealized American landscape under a twilight sky streaked with Art Deco geometry.

entries.title: Campbell's Soup, 1955
entries.description: Long before Andy Warhol immortalized the can, this 1955 magazine spread celebrated "21 delicious varieties" with a cheerful homemaker ladling steaming soup for her rosy-cheeked family. The red-and-white label was already an American icon.

entries.title: Burma-Shave Highway Signs, 1940
entries.description: From 1927 to 1963, sequential roadside rhymes made Burma-Shave brushless shaving cream a beloved motoring institution. This 1940 sequence: "Does your husband / Misbehave / Grunt and Grumble / Rant and Rave? / Shoot the brute some / Burma-Shave."

entries.title: Pan American Airways, 1960
entries.description: As the Jet Age dawned, Pan Am's travel posters promised exotic destinations made newly accessible. This one features a stylized globe and the bold claim: "Pan Am flies to 86 countries" — a vision of the shrinking world.

entries.title: Kelvin-Ator Refrigerator, 1928
entries.description: "No more ice bills!" declares this early electric refrigerator ad. The gleaming white cabinet stands in a pristine kitchen while a smartly dressed housewife marvels at shelves of fresh food — modernity delivered to the American home.

entries.title: Wrigley's Spearmint Gum, 1919
entries.description: This vivid chromolithograph shows the iconic Wrigley Spearmint Spearmen — tiny armored knights on horseback — marching in formation across a giant foil-wrapped stick of gum. The surreal campaign ran for decades.
